items.myth: Sets are ordered (preserve insertion order like dicts).
items.reality: Only dicts gained the insertion-order guarantee in 3.7. Sets remain unordered.
items.example:
```python
s = set()
for x in [3, 1, 2]:
    s.add(x)
list(s)   # could be [1, 2, 3] or [3, 1, 2] — don't rely on it
```

items.reality: `{}` is an empty dict. To make an empty set you must write `set()`.
items.example:
```python
type({})        # <class 'dict'>
type(set())     # <class 'set'>
type({1, 2})    # <class 'set'> — needs at least one element
```

items.myth: A set can contain any objects.
items.reality: Elements must be hashable. Mutable containers (list, dict, set) raise `TypeError: unhashable type` when added.
items.example:
```python
set([[1, 2]])       # TypeError
set([(1, 2)])       # OK — tuples are hashable
```

items.reality: `set` is mutable; `frozenset` is immutable and *hashable*, which is what lets it be a dict key or a member of another set.
items.example:
```python
cache = {}
cache[frozenset({'a','b'})] = 'route-X'   # OK
# cache[{'a','b'}] = ...                  # TypeError: unhashable
```
